name: Fortified Soy Milk
bestFor: Tea, coffee, cereal, smoothies, baking, and higher-protein dairy replacement
nutrition: Typically 80-110 kcal, 6-8 g protein, 250-350 mg calcium, and added vitamin B12 and vitamin D when fortified. Choose unsweetened versions when possible.
recipes: Masala chai with fortified soy milk
Spinach smoothie with banana and flax
buyingTips: Look for unsweetened fortified cartons with at least 6 g protein per cup, calcium near 300 mg, and B12 listed on the nutrition panel.
notes: A practical everyday replacement for dairy milk because soy naturally brings more protein than most almond, rice, or coconut milks. Shake well so added calcium is evenly distributed.

name: Plant Protein Powder
bestFor: Convenient protein top-ups for training days, busy breakfasts, and low-protein meals
nutrition: Most pea, soy, or pea-rice blends provide 20-27 g protein, 100-140 kcal, and 1-4 g carbohydrates per scoop depending on sweeteners and flavors.
recipes: Protein smoothie with banana and peanut butter
buyingTips: Prefer brands that publish third-party testing, list protein per serving clearly, and avoid proprietary blends if you need predictable macros.
notes: Use powder as a convenience food, not a replacement for beans, lentils, tofu, tempeh, whole grains, nuts, and seeds. Soy and pea-rice blends are often the most versatile for amino-acid coverage and texture.

name: Masala Oats
bestFor: Fast savory breakfasts or snacks when paired with extra beans, tofu, or vegetables
nutrition: Often 150-220 kcal with 4-7 g protein per packet before add-ins. Sodium can vary widely, so check labels if you use it often.
recipes: Masala oats with peas and tofu cubes
buyingTips: Choose packets with recognizable spices, lower sodium, and no dairy ingredients. Add legumes or tofu to turn it into a complete meal.
notes: The base food is useful, but instant versions can be salty and light on protein. Treat them as a quick grain base rather than a complete meal by themselves.

name: Peanut Curd
bestFor: Indian-style curd rice, raita, kadhi-style dishes, marinades, and tangy chutneys
nutrition: Homemade nutrition depends on dilution. Peanut curd is usually higher in fat than soy curd and can offer modest protein, magnesium, and vitamin E.
ingredients: Plant-based starter culture or previous batch curd
popularBrands: Plant-based starter cultures from Cultures for Health or similar suppliers
buyingTips: If buying prepared curd, check for added dairy cultures, gelatin, or milk solids. If making at home, use clean jars and refrigerate promptly after setting.
notes: A useful dairy-curd replacement with a rich mouthfeel. People with peanut allergy should choose soy, cashew, coconut, or oat curd alternatives instead.
